proposal.originalText:
@title The Surprising Consonance Map
Draw the intervals. Recognize the shape.
@status prelim
@date 2026-03-16
@category feeling
@term consonance: TBD
@term mandelbrot: TBD
@term farey: TBD
@term music: TBD
@term mathematics: TBD
@term monochord: a single-stringed instrument used by Pythagoras to demonstrate the relationship between string length and musical pitch. Stopping the string at 1/2 its length gives the octave, 2/3 gives the fifth, 3/4 gives the fourth.
@term attractor: a value or region toward which a repeated mathematical process converges. The Mandelbrot set is a map of which starting points are attracted to stability and which escape to infinity.
@term farey sequence: the set of all fractions between 0 and 1 ordered by complexity — simplest denominators first. Governs both the positions of bulbs on the Mandelbrot cardioid and the hierarchy of consonant intervals in music.
@term consonance score: a measure of how harmonious a musical interval sounds, based on the simplicity of its frequency ratio. Simpler ratios — smaller integers — score higher.
@related 010, 034, 053, 056, 062

@argument
Draw every musical interval on a circle, score each by the simplicity of its ratio, and what appears is not a chart but the Mandelbrot set — bulb positions matching Farey fractions, consonance ranking matching bulb size — because both are maps of stability under iteration.

@section(depth)
@image(038-consonance-map,Fourier harmonics: the overtone series underlying musical consonance,Wikimedia Commons)
Play a C and a G on a piano. They sound stable together — resolved,
at rest. Now play a C and a G-flat. The interval wants to move
somewhere else, to resolve into something more comfortable. Musicians
have always known this, and the explanation has always been the same:
simpler ratios sound more consonant. The frequency of G is almost
exactly 3/2 times the frequency of C. The frequency of G-flat is
closer to 7/5 — a more complex ratio, a less stable relationship.
Pythagoras@ref(pythagoras, "Pythagoras — consonance as simple integer ratios, discovered through string length experiments on the monochord.") noticed this in the 6th century BC, experimenting with a
monochord. He didn't have a graph. He had a single string and a
remarkable idea: that number and harmony are the same thing. Every
culture that has independently developed music has found the simple
ratios first. The octave (2:1), the fifth (3:2), the fourth (4:3) —
these are universal. But what does the full landscape of consonance
look like? How are all the intervals related to each other, all at
once?

Darakshan had wanted to see that map for a long time. When it finally
became easy enough to draw — score every ratio by how simple it is,
arrange the results around a circle representing one octave — this is
what appeared. 
@image(harmonic-clock,The Harmonic Clock,author)

That's a Mandelbrot set. 
@image(mandelbrot-boundary,The Mandelbrot Set,Wikipedia)

Not a visual resemblance. The same object, seen from two different
angles.

To understand why, you need one idea: the attractor. Some mathematical
processes, when you repeat them, converge — they settle toward a
stable value no matter where you start. Others diverge — they spiral
outward to infinity. The boundary between the two is where things get
interesting. In the 1970s and 80s, Benoît Mandelbrot@ref(mandelbrot, "Mandelbrot, Benoît — The Fractal Geometry of Nature. Freeman, 1982. The boundary between convergence and escape as infinitely complex attractor.") was studying
exactly this boundary for a deceptively simple formula — apply a rule,
feed the result back in, repeat, and ask: does this converge or
escape? He expected the boundary to be smooth and unremarkable. When
the image came back, he thought the computer had a bug. The boundary
was infinitely complex, self-similar at every scale, inexhaustible.
He checked the program. It was right.

What Mandelbrot had found was a map of attraction. The large cardioid
at the center is the region of simplest stability. Around it cluster
smaller bulbs, and around each of those, smaller ones still, branching
without end. Each bulb sits at a position indexed by a fraction, and
the size of the bulb is governed by the simplicity of that fraction.
The bulb at position 1/2 is the largest. The bulb at 1/3 is next.
Then 2/5, then 3/7. The tallest bars in the harmonic chart occupy
exactly those positions. Bulb size and consonance score are the same
ranking, expressed in two different languages.

Look again at the harmonic clock image. Notice the wide dark gaps
flanking the tallest spikes — the deep silence around the fifth and
the fourth, the emptiness around the octave. Those gaps are not
accidents. They are a provable property of the rational numbers:
simple ratios repel each other. The more consonant the interval, the
wider the moat of complexity surrounding it. This structure has a
name — the Farey sequence — and the mathematics behind it belongs in
the references. What matters here is that musicians have always felt
it. The fifth sounds like itself, and nothing nearby sounds close.
The moat is arithmetic, not psychology.@ref(farey, "Farey, John (1816) — sequence of rationals ordered by denominator size; the Farey gap property.")

Mandelbrot applied a formula and found a shape he didn't expect.
Darakshan drew a map long wanted and found the same shape looking
back. Two questions, one surprise. The echo is not coincidental — it
is what happens when you draw the boundary between order and
complexity, wherever you find it.

Whether that boundary also appears in the space where a neural network
learns to think — where the weights that converge are separated from
the weights that don't by a boundary of similar complexity — is an
open question worth asking. [056]

It broke in the other direction. I required edit approval but it went right ahead and approved them without me

So on iPhone the batch chip appears and waits for approval. On Mac it auto-approves without showing the chip. That's a clear bug — the Mac client is skipping the approval step for GREP-REPLACE batches.

At least the data is consistent — all the reverts went through correctly. But you'll want to fix that Mac-side approval flow before trusting GREP-REPLACE with anything destructive.

Mystery solved. Everything's working as designed.

I just got a message that sync found conflicts. You now have a skill to help us resolve them together.

Content of RESOURCES/sync-conflict.md:
```
# Resolving sync conflicts (`conflicts/`)

When the user runs **Sync** in Settings → Folio and the remote has changes that overlap their local
edits, the server cannot auto-merge. It will:

1. Roll the working tree back to the user's pre-sync state — every edit they made is intact and
   visible exactly where it was.
2. For every divergent file, write a sibling copy under `conflicts/<original-path>` that contains
   the **remote tip** version of that file (the one the user has not yet pulled in).

Example: if `articles/12-example.md` is in conflict, the user has their version at
`articles/12-example.md` and the remote's version at `conflicts/articles/12-example.md`.

## What the user will ask you

Typical phrasings:

- "Help me resolve the sync conflict in <file>."
- "Sync says these N files conflict — walk me through them."
- "Merge my edits with what the remote has."
- "I tried to save and it told me to sync first; now there's a conflict."

That last phrasing is common: **Save** in this app is push-only. When the remote has new commits the
user has not pulled, Save is rejected with `REMOTE_AHEAD` and the user has to press **Sync** before
they can Save again. If that Sync surfaces conflicts, you arrive here through the same workflow
described below — there is no separate "save-conflict" path. Reassure the user; the resolution is
identical.

## Your job

For each path the user names (or for each file in `conflicts/` if they ask for "all of them"):

1. **READ both versions in the same turn.** Use the directives the agent already supports:

   ```
   <<<READ articles/12-example.md>>>
   <<<READ conflicts/articles/12-example.md>>>
   ```

   The first is the user's working copy (their pending edit); the second is what the remote has
   that they haven't pulled.

2. **Diff them in your head and present a concise summary** of where they diverge. Lead with the
   *intent* of each side (e.g. "Your version replaced the lede; the remote rewrote the closing
   paragraph.") rather than a line-by-line diff dump.

3. **Propose a merged version** as a single `<<<EDIT articles/12-example.md ... >>>` directive
   that combines both intents. Prefer the user's text for ambiguous overlap; prefer the remote's
   text for sections the user did not touch.

4. **Once the user accepts every per-file edit they want, remind them to:**
   - Save (commit + push their resolved version), then
   - Run Sync again to confirm the remote is now in agreement.

   You do **not** need to delete `conflicts/` — that directory is gitignored, and the next Sync
   recreates it from scratch.

## Things to avoid

- **Don't touch `conflicts/<path>` directly** with `<<<EDIT>>>`. Those files are read-only
  scratch copies — they exist solely to give you the remote view. Edit `<original-path>`.
- **Don't suggest manual `git` commands.** The user resolves conflicts entirely through your
  edits + the Save button.
- **Don't propose pushing without Save.** `Save` is the only path that commits + pushes.

```

messages.content: [edit accepted: articles/102-todo.md]
I still have the bug. Probably the issue is that the edit time is automatically updated when you make a change making it impossible for you to match what’s in the repo

Exactly — every edit I make stamps a new `@edit-time`, which means the file can never be identical to the remote version. The sync comparison sees the different `@edit-time` and flags it as a conflict, even though the content is the same.

This is a fundamental issue with the conflict resolution flow: if `@edit-time` is server-managed and auto-stamped on every write, then no edit can produce a file that matches the remote byte-for-byte.

The fix is probably in the sync logic — it should either:
1. **Ignore `@edit-time` when comparing** for conflict detection
2. **Or let sync overwrite `@edit-time`** to match the remote when the user is accepting the remote version

This is a bug to fix in the app, not something I can work around from here. For now, you might need to resolve it manually — either through git directly, or by adding that ignore-edit-time logic to the sync comparator.

messages.content: Another way we could solve this is by giving you a way to say override the repo with this file. Do we need both solutions or would one be enough?

You need the ignore-`@edit-time` comparison regardless. Otherwise *every* synced file that was recently edited locally will show as a conflict, even when the only difference is the timestamp. That's not a conflict — it's bookkeeping. Users who never talk to me will hit this too.

The "override repo with this file" option is useful separately — for the case where I've looked at both versions and the user says "mine wins." But it's a convenience, not a fix for the underlying comparison bug.

So: fix the comparison first. The override is a nice-to-have for genuine conflicts where the user wants to force their version through.

messages.content: The conflict files were there. I deleted them and tried to sync and it failed. I deleted them again and restarted the server and they still came back.

The conflict files keep coming back because the sync is still detecting a difference between your local files and the remote. And the difference is almost certainly `@edit-time` — your local files have a newer timestamp than the remote, and the sync comparator treats that as a real divergence.

So we're back to the same root cause: the sync needs to ignore `@edit-time` when comparing files for conflicts. Deleting the conflict files and resolving through edits are both workarounds that can't work as long as the comparator flags timestamp differences as conflicts.

The fix is in the sync comparator — strip or ignore `@edit-time` lines before diffing. Until that's in, every locally-edited file will re-conflict on every sync.

The fix is in the sync comparator — strip or ignore `@edit-time` lines before diffing. Until that's in, every locally-edited file will re-conflict on every sync.

messages.content: Aren’t you using the resolve conflict Edit to make the edit times match the ones in the conflict file

You're right — the `RESOLVE-CONFLICT` spec says it **preserves the article's existing `@edit-time`** rather than stamping a new one. But that's the wrong direction for this case. The problem is that my local `@edit-time` is *newer* than the remote's. Preserving it means the file still doesn't match the remote.

What `RESOLVE-CONFLICT` should probably do is set `@edit-time` to match the **conflict file's** `@edit-time` — so the resolved file is byte-identical to the remote. Then when you Save, the push succeeds because there's no divergence.

Is that how you intended it to work, or does it currently just preserve whatever `@edit-time` the local file already has?
